text: Go. The contents of this chapter are of a more ancient date than those of the foregoing chapter; for the order of time is not always observed in the writings of the prophets. (Challoner) (Worthington)<hr>King Joakim, who succeeded Sellum, the year of the world 3394. (Usher)

Myself, having none greater, Hebrews 6:13. Oaths evince the insincerity of men, who distrust each other; but when God condescends to use them, it shews men's incredulity.<hr>House, the temple, or rather the palace, where the king was sitting at the gate, ver. 2. (Calmet)

Galaad, the head of Libanus. By Galaad, a rich and fruitful country, is here signified the royal palace of the kings of the house of David; by Libanus, a high mountain abounding in cedar-trees, the populous city of Jerusalem. (Challoner) (Worthington) (Theodoret)<hr>Both mountains are connected. (St. Jerome) (Strabo xvi.)<hr>Yet the proper Galaad was at some distance, and on the side of Ammon very barren. The palaces had arisen from a miserable state to resemble Libanus. They shall surely be destroyed. (Calmet)<hr>The advantageous situation of Jerusalem should not protect it, no more than it had done Galaad, 4 Kings 15:29. (Junius; Grotius)

Prepare. Literally, sanctify; (Challoner) as various religious ceremonies were used before the declaration of war, and to know what would be the success. (Calmet)<hr>The Chaldeans were destined to execute God's decrees. (St. Jerome)<hr>Cedars; fine buildings, (ver. 14.; Calmet) and chiefs. (Haydock)

Dead. He means the good king Josias, who by death was taken away, so as not to see the miseries of his country.<hr>Away. Sellum, alias Joachaz, who was carried captive into Egypt, (Challoner) ver. 12., and 4 Kings 23:33. (Calmet)<hr>He was the fourth son of Josias, (1 Paralipomenon 3:15.) and had probably the title of king after the death of Sedecias. (Worthington)
Friend. Joakim forced his subjects to work for him for nothing.

Wide. Hebrew, "measured;" large, and well-proportioned.<hr>Vermilion, (cynopide.) This colour was invented in the town of Pontus, and was used for statues, etc. (Pliny, [Natural History?] 33:6., and 35:6.) (Wisdom 13:14.) (Calmet)<hr>Hebrew shashar (Haydock) may mean indigo. (Junius) (Calmet)
Cedar; Josias. (Calmet)<hr>Shall thy magnificent palaces secure thy empire? (Haydock)<hr>Him. Obey the Lord, in like manner, that thou mayst prosper.
Sister. They shall not condole with his consort.

Jerusalem. Jeremias 36:30. He died indeed with or like his fathers, 4 Kings 24:6. But it is not said that he was buried. (St. Jerome)<hr>The Chaldeans designed to send him to Babylon, (2 Paralipomenon 36:6.) but slew him on the road, (Usher, the year of the world 3405) or treated his corpse ignominiously after his arrival. (Grotius)
Go; Jerusalem. The verbs are feminine.<hr>Lovers; citizens, or Egyptians, etc., Jeremias 27:2.
Pastors, or princes, whose expectations of aid will be frustrated, Osee 12:1. Chaldean, "Thy leaders shall be scattered to all the winds."

Jechonias: Joachin, (4 Kings 24:6.; Worthington) or Chenias, who did not take warning by his predecessor's misfortune.<hr>Ring, or seal, if he were most dear to me, the prime minister of the greatest monarch, Aggeus 2:24. (Calmet)<hr>Alexander the Great gave his ring to Perdiccas, designating him his successor, or regent. (Curt. x.)
Mother; Nohesta, (4 Kings 24:8.) who perverted, (Calmet) or did not correct him in his youth. (Haydock)
Mind; ardently desiring and expecting to be liberated. (Calmet)

Jechonias. Protestants, "Koniah, a despised broken idol? is he," etc. Alexandrian Septuagint, "Jechonias has been dishonoured as a vessel, or which there is no need, for he hath been snatched and cast," etc. (Haydock)

Write this man barren. That is childless: not that he had no children, but that his children should never sit on the throne of Juda, (Challoner) ver. 28., Matthew 1:12., and Ezechiel 17:24. Zorobabel was of his seed, yet he never obtained the title or sovereign power of a king. The Machabees were of a different family. (St. Jerome)<hr>Joachin, or Jechonias, was restored indeed to some favour, (4 Kings 25:27.) but not to the kingdom, no more than any of his posterity, till Christ. (Worthington)
